coredata.dc:title: Chemical Modification of Aluminum Nitride Nanotubes (AlNNT) using-OH, C=O, R-SH functional groups: First Principle's study
coredata.dc:creator: Abinaya, V.
John Thiruvadigal, D.
Akash, R.
Sakthi Balaji, A.
Hariharan, R.M.
Sneha, J.
Adharsh, U
Janani Sivasankar, K.
coredata.dc:description: Based on the First Principle's density functional theory, we have investigated the structural and electronic properties of pristine aluminum nitride nanotube (AlNNT), which is chemically modified with various biologically available functional groups such as hydroxyl (-OH), thiol (R-SH) and carbonyl (C=O). The structural stability is examined through minimum total energy difference and binding energy by repeated random perturbation. Structural analysis shows that functional groups were found to induce sp3 transition in AlNNT as reflected in the p character. Our finding shows that, (5, 5) AlNNT possesses a wide band gap semiconducting behavior with an energy bandgap of 3.27 eV. Chemical functionalization reduces the band gap of armchair AlNNT (5, 5). The chemical potential of the pristine AlNNT is found to be -3.94 eV. Chemical potential analysis shows a blue shift for the bio complex system with respect to the pristine system. The ionic character and Gibbs free energy of solvation determine the structure's solubility in aqueous media. Chemical tuning of AlNNT with functional groups induces polar covalent characteristics which makes them to be used as possible carriers for drug delivery applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Aluminum nitride nanotubes
Density functional theory
Chemical modification
Electronic properties
Nanocarrier

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Special Section on 7th International Conference on Nanoscience and Nanotechnology-ICONN 2023

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Band Structure and Density of States (DOS)
Binding Energy, Total Energy, Formation Energy and Chemical Potential
Partial Charge and Bond Dipole Moment
P character, Ionic Character and Gibb's Free Energy of Solvation
Charge Density Difference (CDD) and Projected Density of States (PDOS)

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support for this work by the DST-FIST, Government of India (Ref. No. SR/FST/PSI-155/2010 ). The authors convey their special thanks to High-Performance Computing Center, SRMIST, for providing the computational facility and to all my beloved teammates.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optimized Structures of (a) AlNNT, (b) AlNNT (-OH), (c) AlNNT (R-SH) and (d) AlNNT (C=O).
Optimized Structures of (a) AlNNT 2(-OH), (b) AlNNT 2(R-SH) and (c) AlNNT 2(C=O).
Band Structure and DOS of Optimized AlNNT.
Band Structure and DOS of Optimized (a) AlNNT (-OH) and (b) AlNNT 2(-OH).
Band Structure and DOS of Optimized (a) AlNNT (R-SH) and (b) AlNNT 2(R-SH).
Band Structure and DOS of Optimized (a) AlNNT (C=O) and (b) AlNNT 2 (C=O).
Charge density difference for (a) AlNNT (b) AlNNT(-OH) (c) AlNNT(R-SH) (d) AlNNT(C=O).
Charge density difference for (a) AlNNT 2(-OH) (b) AlNNT 2(R-SH) (c) AlNNT 2(C=O).
Projected density of states for (a) AlNNT (b) AlNNT (-OH) (c) AlNNT(R-SH) (d) AlNNT(C=O).
Projected density of states for (a) AlNNT 2(-OH) (b) AlNNT 2(R-SH) (c) AlNNT 2(C=O).

Total Energy (eV), Bond Length(Å), Bond Angle (0), s Character (%), p Character (%), Chemical Potential (eV), Energy Bandgap (eV), Binding Energy (eV), Partial Charge (e), Percentage Ionic Character (%), Bond Dipole Moment (D), Gibbs Free Energy of Solvation (kJ/mol) for Single Functionalization.

Total Energy (eV), Bond Length (Å), Bond Angle (0), s Character (%), p Character (%), Chemical Potential (eV), Energy Bandgap (eV), Binding Energy (eV), Partial Charge (e), Percentage Ionic Character (%), Bond Dipole Moment (D), Gibbs Free Energy of Solvation (kJ/mol) for Double Functionalization.

head.ce:title: Chemical Modification of Aluminum Nitride Nanotubes (AlNNT) using-OH, C=O, R-SH functional groups: First Principle's study

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics and Nanotechnology, SRM Institute of Science and Technology, Kattankulathur, 603203, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: SRM Institute of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics and Nanotechnology, SRM Institute of Science and Technology, Kattankulathur, 603203, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Based on the First Principle's density functional theory, we have investigated the structural and electronic properties of pristine aluminum nitride nanotube (AlNNT), which is chemically modified with various biologically available functional groups such as hydroxyl (-OH), thiol (R-SH) and carbonyl (C=O). The structural stability is examined through minimum total energy difference and binding energy by repeated random perturbation. Structural analysis shows that functional groups were found to induce sp3 transition in AlNNT as reflected in the p character. Our finding shows that, (5, 5) AlNNT possesses a wide band gap semiconducting behavior with an energy bandgap of 3.27 eV. Chemical functionalization reduces the band gap of armchair AlNNT (5, 5). The chemical potential of the pristine AlNNT is found to be -3.94 eV. Chemical potential analysis shows a blue shift for the bio complex system with respect to the pristine system. The ionic character and Gibbs free energy of solvation determine the structure's solubility in aqueous media. Chemical tuning of AlNNT with functional groups induces polar covalent characteristics which makes them to be used as possible carriers for drug delivery applications.

ce:list-item.ce:para: The data that support the research findings of this study are not publicly available due to confidentiality of the information, data protection and privacy, and they can be provided upon reasonable request through the proper channel.

ce:section.ce:para: One-dimensional (1D) nanowires and nanotubes have various applications in science and technology due to their unique and fascinating chemical, electrical, optical and thermal properties [1]. The discovery of CNT sparked an increase in theoretical and experimental research. It has been shown that the chirality and tube diameter of carbon nanotubes determine whether they are metallic or semiconducting [2]. The majority of carbon nanotubes that have been found consists of numerous coaxial tubes, with the spacing between their walls resembling the interplanar distance of graphite. The properties of multi-walled carbon nanotubes are projected to be much more complex due to their sensitivity to the exact geometry of each individual tube [3].

In comparison to 0 D materials, 1 D nanomaterials have larger surface area and more exposed sites spread on their surfaces. The excellent structural, physical and chemical properties of 1 D materials favors researchers to use them for gas sensing applications. The high length to diameter ratio of 1 D nanomaterials with fast charge transfer enhances sensitivity and response speed of the material to be used in gas sensing applications [4]. The morphological and semiconducting properties tunable with number of layers together with high surfaced volume ratio of 2 D sheets makes it a promising candidate for gas sensing applications. Novel stanene based nanotubes and nanosheets have been theoretically designed to be used as capable candidates in gas sensor devices [5–10].

An aluminum Nitride (AlN) nanotube (NT) is a structural counterpart of a carbon nanotube (CNT) in nature. A carbon nanotube (CNT) in which the C atom has completely been replaced with an alternative set of Al and N atoms can be used to visualize an aluminum nitride nanotube (AlNNT), which can be seen as a rolled-up hexagonal AlN layer. Depending on their crystal lattice orientation, AlN can have either a zigzag or an armchair structural shape [11]. The unique electronic and mechanical characteristics together with wide bandgap, high chemical and thermal stability, and sharper electrical sensitivity caught the attention of the researchers towards inorganic aluminum nitride nanotubes. Aluminum nitride nanotubes has a higher energy of adsorption and reactivity with considerable polarity in comparison to other nanostructures [12]. The non-carbon AlNNT are a highly interesting material and more suitable than other nano structures for sensing and biological applications like drug delivery [13,14].

Numerous kinds of research have described the synthesis of AlN nanotubes using the highly non-equilibrium dc-arc plasma method [15]. Because of their outstanding thermal conductivity, AlN nanotubes, for instance, have been suggested to be used in high-performance field-effect transistors and thermoelectric devices [16]. In addition, their potential applications as catalysts in chemical reactions and hydrogen storage has been investigated [17].

Chemical modification of low dimensional nanostructures such as nanosheets, nanotubes and nano dots involves altering their surface properties or incorporating new functionalities. The reactivity, interaction and solubility of nano structures with other substances can also be altered by attaching functional groups to their surface. This can be accomplished using organic or inorganic compounds that react with the surface of the nanotubes, creating covalent or noncovalent bonding interactions. In accordance with Hazem Abdelsalama et al. [18] studies on silicene, graphene and hexagonal boron quantum dots in armchair and zigzag configuration, the 2 D structures were chemically modified at the edges using functional groups like hydroxyl, cyano, nitro, amide, isopropyl, carboxylic acid and elements like H, F, O and S respectively. It is found that these chemically modified structures are thermodynamically stable under edge relaxation. The chemical functionalization of 2 D materials leads to spontaneous structure transition and acts as a powerful tool in tuning the electronic and optical properties of graphene, silicene and hexagonal boron quantum dots. It was found that the chemical functionalization has profound impact on the electronic properties of 2 D nanostructures. The energy band gap strongly depends on shape of the material and functional moiety. A reduction in band gap is observed with increase in concentration of functional group. These chemically modified 2 D materials have widespread applications in nanoelectronics, biosensors, supercapacitors, nanomedicines, transistors, thermoelectrics and gas sensors [19,20].

Chemical modification is a very efficient tool to tune the properties of nanosystems toward different applications. Studies by Wei Hu et al. [21] on the 2 D phosphorene material whose edges are modified by passivation with hydrogen /fluorine to construct a heterojunction from a single type of material. The computational results of the proposed edge modified heterojunction shows a reduction in HOMO/LUMO energy gap due to opposite shifts of HOMO and LUMO energy levels. The localization of HOMO or LUMO states in the donor and acceptor region indicates creation of opposite charges at the interface of heterojunction. The heterojunction shows good optical absorption properties with maximum power conversion efficiency of 20% competitive with other 2 D heterojunction. Furthermore, studies carried out by Hazem Abdelsalam et al. [22] to investigate the electronic and optical properties of silicene, graphene and arsenene hetero structure through chemical functionalization and shape modification reveals a very low conduction band offset 0.002 eV, low energy gap (1.16 eV) and efficiency 23.34% for the AHEX-Si-3–2H/ZHEX-G-F hetero structure. Low energy excitation confirmed by the optical absorption spectra suggest the possibility of using edge modified 2 D heterojunction for the constructions of type II solar cells.

AlNNT are a promising wide band-gap material with high reactivity and sensitivity towards chemical functionalization moiety. Many theoretical studies have suggested potential applications of AlNNT in hydrogen storage, chemical sensor and gas absorbent. Research on chemical modification of AlNNT is still at the earlier stage with few papers related to their synthesis [23]. Experimental reports by Gaweł Zyła et al. [24] suggests the synthesis of aluminum nitride – ethylene glycol nanofluids. The dynamic viscosity, thermal conductivity and electrical conductivity were measured at a constant temperature of 298.12 K. The suspension exhibits a non-Newtonian nature with an increase in thermal and electrical conductivity with increase in the concentration of nanoparticles in the suspension. Experimental investigation by Qiqiu Huanga et al. [25], on the flexible SBS/PA/AlN phase change materials (PCM) envisages maximum temperature control below 32.5 °C with an optimal 15% AlN mass fraction to improve the heat dissipation for battery modules. The flexible PCM absorbs heat generated by batteries by improving thermal homogeneity and prevention thermal runaway propagation. These newly designed PCM systems provides a guide line while designing effective thermal management system.

Carbohydrates, lipids, proteins, and nucleic acids, hydroxyl groups (-OH) can be found in a variety of biomolecules. Cancer imaging and treatment have utilized hydroxylated nanoparticles such as iron oxide and gold nanoparticles [26].

Amino acids are building blocks of proteins and human life. As an amino acid in proteins and a vital component of the creation of disulfide connections between proteins, cysteine, which has a thiol group, is essential. As prodrugs that are activated when cancer cells have high concentrations of reactive oxygen species (ROS), thiols can be employed in the treatment of cancer [27].

An oxygen atom and a carbon atom are double-bonded (C=O) to form, a functional group carbonyl. The Carbonyl group is helpful in a wide range of chemical reactions as well as biological processes due to its key chemical characteristics. Carbonyl groups, which are essential to a number of chemotherapy medicines are found in abundance. Doxorubicin's capacity to intercalate into DNA and prevent DNA replication depends on the carbonyl group in the medication [28].

In several areas of cancer research and treatment, including drug development, diagnosis, and therapy, functional groups, and their derivatives offer potential usefulness and are crucial chemicals for understanding cancer because they play a key role in a variety of biological functions that are changed in cancer cells. Few studies have been carried out on the functionalization of AlNNT using O2, HCOH, and NH3 molecules [29–31].

In the present work, DFT studies were carried out on armchair AlNNTs (5, 5) functionalized with hydroxyl (-OH), thiol (R-SH), and carbonyl (C=O) functional groups on their surfaces to change their structure and electronic properties. The pristine and functionalized AlNNT structures [AlNNT-(X/2X), where X=-OH, R-SH, C=O] were investigated for their structural stability. Functionalization of AlNNT, may increase their solubility, biocompatibility, and reactivity and make them more suitable as nanocarriers to be used in drug delivery applications.

In this study, all the calculations were performed by using Density Functional Theory (DFT) with generalized gradient approximation (GGA) - Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) [32] set as exchange-correlation energy functional with FHI pseudopotential and double zeta polarized basis set [33]. The energy mesh cut-off value was fixed as 250 Rydberg. The Brillouin zone was sampled by using the k-points 1 × 1 × 120. All the geometry optimization of the three functional groups and armchair (5, 5) AlNNTs were carried out individually by using the Quantum ATK package [34]. Similarly, AlNNT (X/2X) nanohybrid complexes were geometrically optimized. The Hellmann-Feynman force tolerance was fixed as 0.05 eV/Å. Convergence criterion for total energy is set as 10− 5 eV and vacuum spacing along x, y and z directions set as (30 Å, 28 Å and 19 Å), respectively. The electron temperature was set to 300 K.

Based on the First principle's density functional theory, we have optimized pristine, single, and double-functionalized single-walled Aluminum Nitride Nanotube (AlNNT) with functional groups such as Hydroxyl (-OH), Thiol (R-SH), and Carbonyl (C=O) (Figs. 1 . and 2 .). The optimized armchair (5, 5) AlNNT with a tube length of 17.46 Å and diameter of 8.6 Å was chosen as the base structure. The averaged bond length for Al-N is found to be 1.81 Å had good agreement with studies carried out by Beheshtian J et al. [35]. For the optimized AlNNT the averaged bond angle is found to be 122. 13°Both, the bond length and bond angle values are reported in Table 1 . Chemical modification of AlN nanotube with various functional groups shows an increment in bond length (Al - S for AlNNT (R-SH)/AlNNT 2(R-SH), Al - C for AlNNT (C=O)/AlNNT 2(C=O)) and decrement in bond length for Al - O for AlNNT (-OH)/AlNNT 2(-OH). The bond angle values for AlNNT(X/2X) structures range between 97° and 109°, predicting a complete transition towards sp3 character.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Band Structure and Density of States (DOS)
ce:section.ce:para: The energy difference between the top of the valence band and the bottom of the conduction band is referred to as the band gap. AlNNT is an indirect wide band gap semiconductor. The bandgap of AlNNT is directly proportional to its diameter [36]. The band structure of pristine AlNNT ranges between ± 1.7 to 4 eV, exhibiting a symmetrical distribution as shown in Fig. 3 . Whereas, in the case of AlNNT (X/2X) structures, with respect to -OH and R-SH functionalization exhibit asymmetrical band structure which is depicted in Fig. 4 . (a, b) and Fig. 5 . (a, b).

For the C=O functionalized AlNNT (5, 5), energy levels shift upwards in the valence band and downwards in the conduction band, retaining the symmetrical distribution displayed in Fig. 6 . (a, b). In comparison to the singly functionalized system (-OH/R-SH), the doubly functionalized system (2 -OH/ 2 R-SH) show an increment in band gap due to the upward shift of conduction band energies. Precisely, functionalization reduces the band gap of armchair AlNNT (5, 5).

Band gap (Eg) of AlNNT is found to be influenced with functionalization due to the quantum confinement effect. The Eg value decreases monotonically based on the nature of functional moiety following the order AlNNT – (C=O/2 C=O) < AlNNT – (R-SH/2 R-SH) < AlNNT - (-OH/2 -OH). The sp3 transition induced by the orbital hybridization between Al-O in AlNNT - (-OH), Al-S in AlNNT – (R-SH) and Al-C in AlNNT – (C=O) leads to the addition of energy levels in the valence band for AlNNT - (-OH/2 -OH) and AlNNT – (R-SH/2 R-SH) systems and reduction of energy levels in the conduction band near the vicinity of Fermi level. In the case of AlNNT – (C=O/2 C=O) system, addition of energy levels both in the valence and conduction band near the Fermi level vicinity with a profound peak for (C=O/2 C=O) energy level appearing in the conduction band at 1.3 eV. Band structural variations of functionalized AlNNT matches exactly with the PDOS studies. The symmetrical band structure of AlNNT in the present study matches with the band gap obtained by M.S. Jothi et al. [37] on the adsorption of chloromethane, bromomethane and phosphorene nanoribbon. Precisely, functionalization reduces the band gap of armchair AlNNT (5, 5) which matches with the studies carried out by Saurabh Kharwar et al. [38] on the cove - edge aluminum nitride nanoribbon showing a change in band gap from semiconductor to metallic transition.

The ratio of surface atoms in the one-dimensional nanostructures determine the valence band maximum energy level and conduction band minimum energy level. The armchair AlNNT (5,5) nanotube in the present study, has its bottom of the conduction energy minima located at the Γ point and the top of the valence energy maxima at ∼1/2 along the ΓZ direction. Moreover, all the computed one-dimensional AlN nanotubes have same trend of smaller bandgaps in comparison to the bulk AlN. This is due to the surface effects dominating and counteracting the quantum size effects [39,40].

The density of states distribution for the one-dimensional armchair (5, 5) AlNNT varies as E−1/2 and matches exactly with the band structure shown in Fig. 3. Functionalization with -OH and R-SH moieties predict an increase in DOS magnitude in the valence band and decrease in DOS magnitude in the conduction band Fig. 4 (a, b) and Fig. 5 (a, b). Whereas, functionalization using C=O moiety predicts an increment in DOS magnitude in both valence and conduction band depicted in Fig. 6 (a, b). These findings suggest the possibility of strong orbital hybridization between the C=O and (5, 5) AlNNT highlighting the formation of a strong polar-covalent bond. The DOS results of the present study indicate a stronger interaction in the AlNNT/C=O complex in comparison to the weak covalent interaction in the NH3/AlNNT complex obtained by Ali Ahmadi et al. [30].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Binding Energy, Total Energy, Formation Energy and Chemical Potential
ce:section.ce:para:
The binding energy and total energy analysis are used to determine the structural stability of the systems. A decrease in total energy and binding energy towards a more negative value indicates thermodynamic stability. The calculated binding energies [41] and total energies [32] of AlNNT/ (X, 2X) are listed in Tables 1 and 2 . The binding energy can be calculated using Eq. (1),
(1)
E
B E
=
{
E
A l N N T −
(
X / 2 X
)
}
−
{
E
A l N N T
+
E
X / 2 X
}

Where, E
AlNNT − (X/2X) – total energy of the nanobiohybrid system, EAlNNT - total energy of the pristine structure and E
X/2X - total energy of single or double functional groups. The total energy of the nano-bio complex is found to be lower than pristine (5, 5) AlNNT indicates stronger interaction in the complex system. The negative binding energy of the bio-nano hybrid system indicates an energetically favorable configuration. The results suggest that the nature of functional moiety intrinsically alters the binding energy of the (5, 5) AlNNT/ (X, 2X) structures. The binding energy results are in accordance with the study carried out by Zabiollah Mahdavifar et al. on AlNNT, SiCNT, and BNNT doped with Cu [42].

Moreover, the formation energy is computed to confirm the structural stability of AlNNT. The formation energy is calculated using Eq. (2)
(2)
E
F
=
1 N
[
E
AlN
−
X
(
E
Al
)
−
Y
(
E
N
)
]
Where
EF -Formation energy
N- Total number of atoms
X, Y- Number of aluminum atoms, Number of nitrogen atoms
EAlN,- Total energy of aluminum nitride nanotube
EAl, EN- Total energy of aluminum atom, total energy of nitrogen atom.
Formation energy of AlNNT comes to be −7.216 eV, which confirms stable formation of the nanotube.

The chemical potential of (5, 5) AlNNT / (X, 2X) is shown in Table 1 and Table 2. The attachment of the -OH/2-OH, R-SH/2R-SH functional group decreases the chemical potential towards the blue wavelength region. In the case of attachment of the C=O/2 C=O functional group, the chemical potential of the AlNNT complex is slightly reduced with respect to pristine AlNNT. The chemical potential analysis of the present study is found to be 0.19 eV lower than the chemical potential obtained for AlNNT for the adsorption studies on AlNNT carried out by Mohsen Doust Mohammadi et al. [43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Partial Charge and Bond Dipole Moment
ce:section.ce:para: The Mulliken partial charges [44] are used to evaluate the degree of polar covalency in a bond. The calculated partial charge and bond dipole moment are summarized in Table 1 and Table 2. Optimized (5, 5) AlNNT is found to be purely covalent with almost zero charge transfer. Whereas, upon functionalization partial charges are created between AlNNT and X/2X structures. The donor or acceptor behavior of a nanohybrid complex is predicted by the positive/negative value of Mulliken partial charges. In the case of AlNNT (-OH), AlNNT 2(-OH) the nanotube acts as the donor and -OH as the acceptor. Whereas, in the case of AlNNT-(R-SH/C=O), AlNNT-2(R-SH/C=O), the nanotube acts as an acceptor and R-SH or C=O as donor. The functionalization using hydroxyl and thiol groups creates lower partial charges < 0.4 e in the AlNNT –(-OH), AlNNT 2(-OH), AlNNT(R-SH) system indicating the formation of the nonpolar covalent bond and higher partial charges >0.4 e in the AlNNT 2(R-SH), AlNNT(C=O), AlNNT 2(C=O) systems, indicate the formation of polar covalent bond.

The bond dipole moment [45] for the optimized (5,5) AlNNT is very low and comes to be 0.017 D. The creation of partial charges induces a bond dipole moment in the (5, 5) AlNNT (X/2X) structures. The bond dipole moment of polar covalent bonds in the functionalized system is found to be much higher than the non-polar covalent bonds.

ce:section.ce:section-title: P character, Ionic Character and Gibb's Free Energy of Solvation
ce:section.ce:para:
Polar covalency of a bond is reflected in the decrease in s character and increase in p character [46] and calculated values are displayed in Table 1 and Table 2. A polar covalent bond has sp3 character, with p character equal to 75%. Covalent modification of (5, 5) AlNNT with functional groups tunes the p character which follows the range AlNNT (C=O/2 C=O) > AlNNT (R-SH/2 R-SH) > AlNNT (-OH/ 2 -OH). The s character can be calculated using Eq. (3)
(3)
s =
1
1 + m
× 100
Where,
m =
− 1
c o s θ
and θ is the average bond angle. The p character can be calculated using Eq. (4),
(4)
100 −
s
c h a r
=
p
c h a r

In the present study, polar covalent bonded systems are found to have higher ionic character in comparison to the non-polar covalently bonded systems. Very high ionic character is obtained for the AlNNT 2(C=O) complex suggests the system to be most favorable.

Solvation process is thermodynamically favorable for the negative values of Gibbs free energy [46]. Double functionalization is found to increase the solvation of nanotube in aqueous media. The ability of a solute to be dissolved in a solvent is computationally calculated using Gibbs free energy of solvation through Born Eq. (5).
(5)
Δ
G = −
N A
z
2
e
2
8 π
ε
0
r 0
(
1 −
1
ε
r
)

Here, NA = Avogadro constant (6.023 × 1023 mol−1), Z = partial charge (e),e 2 = elementary charge (1.6 × 10−19C), ε0 = permittivity of free space (8.854 × 10−12 F/m),r 0 = bond length (Å), ε r = dielectric constant of water (77.2 in 301 K). In the present result, a higher value of solvation energy obtained for AlNNT 2(C=O) suggest the preferential aqueous solubility of nanotubes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Charge Density Difference (CDD) and Projected Density of States (PDOS)
ce:section.ce:para: The difference between the self-consistent valence charge density and the superposition of atomic valence densities is called as charge density difference (CDD) or electron difference density. The charge density difference for the validation of chemical bonding is plotted with an isovalue of 0.08 Å −3. CDD is positive on N (more electrons-red area) and negative on Al (less electron-blue area). The CDD plot (Fig. 7 and Fig. 8 ) shows that aluminum atoms act as most favourable site for attraction of nucleophile. The CDD plot functionalization study carried out on AlNNT by Zahra Rostami et al.[47], and effect of doping on the stanine nanosheet to use them as novel gas sensing materials carried out by Amirali Abbasi et al. [9,48,49] matches with the present results of chemisorption.

The projected density of states (PDOS) of AlNNT shows that the electron rich nitrogen contributes maximum to the valence band and electron deficiency aluminum contributes maximum to the conduction band. The effect of functional groups (-OH, R-SH, C=O) on the electronic properties of AlNNT are validated using PDOS spectrum as shown in Fig. 9 and Fig. 10 . Functional group induces orbital hybridization with higher contribution of AlNNT in the valence band. Functional groups have higher contribution in valence band than in the conduction band except for the case of C=O functional group which has a strong peak at energy 1.3 eV in the conduction band. The intensity of functional group interaction with AlNNT increases with increase in their concentration as shown in the Fig. 9 and Fig. 10. Increase in number of C=O peaks is observed in the conduction band for higher concentration. The results of PDOS predict effective bonding interaction between the functional group and AlNNT are in agreement with the studies carried out by Zahra Rostami et al. [47] on the interaction of nitrotyrosine with aluminum nitride nanostructures.

Interestingly, the position and nature of functional group tunes the covalent character in the bond Al-O, Al- S and Al-C. Polar covalency is achieved in the case of AlNNT C=O, AlNNT 2 (C=O) and AlNNT 2 (R-SH). Lowering of band gap favours higher reactivity in the bionano hybrid system. An ionic character of 58% obtained for AlNNT 2(C=O) with higher negative solvation energy recommends its possible usage as nanocarriers in drug delivery applications.

First principle density functional theory was used to optimize (5, 5) AlNNT and functionalize the armchair (5, 5) AlNNT with functional groups such as hydroxyl (-OH), thiol (R-SH) and carbonyl (C=O). The geometrical properties such as bond angles for AlNNT(X/2X) structures ranges between 97° and 109° and p character greater than or equal to 75% a predict complete transition towards sp3 character. The negative binding energy and total energy value indicates thermodynamic stability of armchair (5, 5) AlNNT. The density of states distribution for the one-dimensional armchair (5, 5) AlNNT varies as E −1/2 and matches exactly with band structure. The partial charges and bond dipole moment supports an increase in hydrophilicity upon functionalization. The results of PDOS and CDD suggests the formation of chemical bonding. The findings of percentage ionic character and Gibbs free energy of solvation predicts the higher solubility of (5, 5) AlNNT 2(C=O) structure in aqueous media. Hence AlNNT 2(C=O) structure could be used as potential nano carriers for targeted drug delivery applications.

We the undersigned declare that this manuscript is original, has not been published before and is not currently being considered for publication elsewhere. We confirm that the manuscript has been read and approved by all named authors and that there are no other persons who satisfied the criteria for authorship but are not listed.
We further confirm that the order of authors listed in the manuscript has been approved by all of us.
We understand that the Corresponding Author is the sole contact for the Editorial process.
He/she is responsible for communicating with the other authors about progress, submissions or revisions.
All authors of this manuscript contributed to this research study.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support for this work by the DST-FIST, Government of India (Ref. No. SR/FST/PSI-155/2010). The authors convey their special thanks to High-Performance Computing Center, SRMIST, for providing the computational facility and to all my beloved teammates.
